name: Futodama-no-Mikoto
shortDescription: Futodama-no-Mikoto is a Shinto deity associated with divination and rituals. He played a vital role in the myth of Amaterasu's concealment in the Ama-no-Iwato cave.
description: Futodama-no-Mikoto is a significant kami (deity) in Shinto mythology, primarily known for his role in the narrative surrounding Amaterasu's retreat into the Ama-no-Iwato (heavenly rock cave). As a skilled diviner and ritualist, Futodama was instrumental in the efforts to lure Amaterasu out of the cave, bringing light and order back to the world. He is often depicted as performing rituals alongside Ame-no-Koyane, another important deity associated with divine pronouncements and the imperial court. Futodama is said to have held and presented the sacred sakaki tree adorned with jewels and a mirror during the ceremony outside the cave, symbolizing the restoration of the world's beauty and order. His association with divination and ritual purification (harae) makes him a central figure in Shinto practices aimed at appeasing the kami and maintaining harmony between the human and divine realms. He is considered an ancestor of the Nakatomi clan, who historically held important positions in the imperial court, overseeing religious ceremonies and serving as advisors to the emperor. His presence is invoked during important Shinto rituals and festivals, emphasizing his continued relevance in contemporary Shinto practice. The shimenawa, a sacred rope used to demarcate sacred spaces, is also associated with him and other deities involved in the Ama-no-Iwato myth.
